user_request: Draft a suit for recovery of Rs.20,00,000/- paid as advance for a business transaction which failed due to Defendant's default. Plead total failure of consideration under Section 65 of Indian Contract Act. Claim refund with interest and costs. Include cause of action paragraph with accrual date, continuing nature of cause of action, valuation, court fee, and proper verification clause. Draft suitable for filing before the District Court.

facts.summary: Plaintiff seeks recovery of Rs. 20,00,000/- paid as advance for a business transaction that failed due to the Defendant's default. The suit alleges total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act and claims a refund with interest and costs.

slots.value: Recovery of money with interest and costs

assumptions: Jurisdiction lies with the District Court based on valuation or cause of action

S. 26. Institution of suits.
91[(1)]Every suit shall be instituted by the presentation of a plaint or in such other manner as may be

[(2) In every plaint, facts shall be proved by affidavit].
The Amending Act of 1999 has inserted a new sub-s (2) in s 26. The old s 26 has been re-numbered as sub-s 
(1). The Amending Act comes into effect from 1 July 2002. It shall not apply to or affect any suit pending 
immediately before the commencement of amending provisions and every such suit shall be tried as if the 
amending provision had not come into force.
The amended s 26 requires that in every plaint, facts shall be proved by an affidavit. The requirement of 
affidavit is not limited only to plaint but extends to the written statement as well, as it appears from the insertion 
of sub-r (3) of r 15 of O 6. The said rule provides that person verifying the pleading shall also furnish an affidavit 
in support of his pleadings.
The Law Commission in its 163rd report strongly proposed the amendment to s 26 in its present format. It was 
thought that the amendment was salutary and may, at least to some

ed by the court.94

money for the purchase of the suit property was paid by the plaintiff. The stand was subsequently changed and it 
was stated that payment was made by the defendant, but, from the plaintiffs fund. The plaintiff and the defendant 
were husband and wife. It was held that the husband effecting act ual operation on behalf of his wife, is not an 
uncommon occurrence. Suit was not liable to be dismissed for changes in the stand of the plaintiff.95
Where the written statement does not raise the plea that the verification of the plaint was not proper, then the plea 
cannot be raised at the time of the trial of the suit.1
Parties cannot give up the case set out in the pleadings and propound a new and different one. The pleadings are 
the foundation of the case.2

(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

sections.heading: BETWEEN
sections.instruction: Draft the parties section listing plaintiff and defendant(s) with full particulars: name, age, occupation, and address. Use sui juris averment for competency. If any detail is missing, use the appropriate {{PLACEHOLDER}}.

sections.heading: JURISDICTION
sections.instruction: Draft the jurisdiction section establishing territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction. For territorial jurisdiction, state SPECIFIC facts: where the Defendant resides or carries on business, where payment was made, where the agreement was executed, or where the cause of action arose — with concrete place references. For pecuniary jurisdiction, state the claim amount and that it falls within this court's monetary limits.

sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
sections.instruction: Draft the facts section as numbered paragraphs in chronological narrative form. Each paragraph must tell a story — not state conclusions. Cover: (1) How the parties came to know each other and the nature of their relationship, (2) What was agreed — the specific terms of the transaction/agreement, (3) What obligations each party undertook and the timeline for performance, (4) How payment was made — specific amount, mode, date, (5) What exactly constituted the defendant's failure — describe the specific default with detail, (6) What happened after the default — communications, demands, legal notice and response. Reference documents as 'Annexure [LABEL]'. Do NOT state legal conclusions in this section — keep it purely factual.

sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
sections.instruction: Draft the legal basis section in paragraph form. For each statutory provision: (1) State what the provision says — the legal rule it creates, (2) Explain what factual conditions trigger its application, (3) Show how the facts of this case satisfy those conditions. If applicable, include an alternative plea (e.g., Section 73 ICA as alternative to Section 65 ICA) using 'In the alternative and without prejudice to the above...' framing. Include defensive pleading: pre-emptively neutralize likely defense arguments (e.g., no part performance was rendered, no forfeiture clause exists, no adjustment made, no counter-claim exists). Cite ONLY provisions from the verified list.

must_include.description: At least one statutory section cited
Legal consequence explained (not just section named)

sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
sections.instruction: Draft the cause of action section in PARAGRAPH prose (not numbered sub-headings). Must cover: (1) ORIGIN — when and how the right to sue first arose, anchored to the default/failure date, (2) FURTHER ACCRUAL — when the cause of action further arose (e.g., failure to comply with legal notice), (3) CONTINUITY — that the cause of action is continuing because the defendant continues to wrongfully retain the money. The cause must be anchored to the failure/default date, NOT the notice date. Write in flowing paragraph form — avoid mechanical sub-headings like '(a) underlying obligation'.

sections.heading: LIMITATION
sections.instruction: Draft the limitation section stating: (1) the applicable Article of the Limitation Act, 1963 Schedule (from enrichment or use placeholder), (2) the limitation period, (3) the starting date of limitation (must be anchored to cause_of_action_date/default_date, NOT notice_date), (4) that the suit is within time. If limitation article is not available in enrichment, use {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} placeholder.

sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
sections.instruction: Draft the valuation and court fee section stating: (1) suit valuation equals the claim amount, (2) court fee calculated per applicable state court fees act. Use court_fee context from web search if available, otherwise use {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} placeholder.

sections.heading: INTEREST
sections.instruction: Draft the interest section with a 3-part structure: (1) Pre-suit interest — from default date to filing date with rate and justification, (2) Pendente lite interest — during pendency of suit under Section 34 CPC with rate, (3) Future interest — post-decree until realization with rate. Justify the interest rate: state why the claimed rate is appropriate (commercial transaction, wrongful retention, prevailing bank rate, contractual understanding). Cite Section 34 of CPC for pendente lite interest.

sections.heading: PRAYER
sections.instruction: Draft the prayer section with at least 6 specific sub-prayers as lettered items (a, b, c, ...). Each prayer must state exact amounts or rate placeholders — never vague. Must include: (a) decree for principal amount with Rs. figure, (b) pre-suit interest with rate and date range, (c) pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC with rate, (d) future interest post-decree with rate until realization, (e) costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs for sending legal notice, (f) any other relief the court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances.

sections.heading: LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON
sections.instruction: Generate a numbered list of documents the plaintiff relies upon, mapping to the annexure scheme. For each document: Serial No., Description, Annexure Label. Only list documents mentioned in evidence. If evidence is empty, list standard expected documents with {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}} placeholders.

sections.heading: VERIFICATION
sections.body:
I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of the above plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and the contents based on legal advice are believed to be true on the basis of such advice. No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

filled_sections.heading: BETWEEN
filled_sections.text: Between Plaintiff and Defendant, Plaintiff {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, files this money recovery action in the court having jurisdiction on the date of filing, by filing this complaint; Plaintiff is sui juris and competent to sue and be sued. Defendant {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, with address at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, is the Defendant in this action; Defendant is sui juris.

filled_sections.heading: JURISDICTION
filled_sections.text:
The Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which lies within the territorial jurisdiction of the District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}. The advance amount of Rs. 20,00,000 was paid by the Plaintiff to the Defendant on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} from the Plaintiff's account at {{BANK_NAME}}, {{BANK_BRANCH}}, and the payment was received by the Defendant at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}. The agreement between the parties was executed on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} at {{AGREEMENT_LOCATION}}. The cause of action arose at {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_LOCATION}} on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} when the Defendant defaulted by failing to repay the advance and perform contractual obligations by {{DEFAULT_DATE}}. The Plaintiff seeks refund of the advance with interest and costs on the basis of total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act. The principal amount claimed is Rs. 2,000,000, i.e., Rs. 20,00,000, which is within the pecuniary limits of this District Court; accordingly, the claim falls within the Court's monetary jurisdiction for suits of such nature.

Place: {{COURT_PLACE}}

filled_sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
filled_sections.text:
1. The Plaintiff and the Defendant were introduced to each other in the course of their business activities and, following preliminary discussions, entered into a formal commercial arrangement on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, as evidenced by the written contract/Agreement dated that day marked Annexure A. The relationship was transactional in nature, with the Plaintiff seeking a commercial output from the Defendant in exchange for an upfront payment, and the Defendant agreeing to provide the corresponding goods or services upon payment.

2. The terms of the Agreement envisaged that the Plaintiff would furnish an advance of Rs. 2,000,000 to the Defendant as consideration for the goods or services to be supplied, with the Defendant undertaking to perform and deliver the contracted goods/services by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} in accordance with the specifications set out in Annexure A. The parties’ intention was that the advance would be applied toward the price of the goods/services and that the Defendant would complete performance within the agreed timeframe.

3. In accordance with the agreed terms, the Plaintiff undertook and completed the advance payment in the amount of Rs. 2,000,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, evidenced by the payment proof annexed as Annexure B. The payment was made as part of the consideration for the performance promised by the Defendant under the Agreement, and the transaction was documented in Annexure A for the Court’s reference.

4. The Defendant undertook to perform by the date specified in the Agreement, namely by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}, and to deliver the agreed goods or services in the manner described in Annexure A. The Plaintiff, relying on the Defendant’s obligation, provided the advance amount in good faith and awaited performance in accordance with the timetable and terms set forth in the contract.

5. By {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant had neither performed the contracted obligations nor refunded the advance payment of Rs. 2,000,000. The Plaintiff contends that performance was not delivered and that the funds were not returned, despite the express obligation to perform by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} and to remedy the breach by returning the advance if performance could not be fulfilled. The Defendant’s default is reflected in the absence of any delivery or refund by that date and the continued failure to satisfy the contractual obligations.

6. After the default, the Plaintiff sought to resolve the matter amicably and through formal channels. On {{NOTICE_DATE}}, the Plaintiff caused a legal notice to be issued to the Defendant demanding refund of Rs. 2,000,000 along with lawful interest and costs, as recorded in Annexure C. The Defendant replied on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} through Annexure D, wherein the Defendant set forth defenses or counter-statements but did not rectify the breach or return the advance amount, leaving the Plaintiff’s claim unresolved and continuing.

7. The Plaintiff subsequently filed the present suit on {{FILING_DATE}} for recovery of Rs. 2,000,000, together with interest and costs, on the ground of total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act and for such other relief as the Court may deem fit. The cause of action first arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}}, when the Defendant’s non-performance and non-refund materialized in relation to the contract and the advance paid by the Plaintiff. Annexure E and Annexure F contain the acknowledgment and supporting documents referenced, as applicable, in the course of these proceedings.

filled_sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
filled_sections.text:
Section 65 Indian Contract Act, 1872 creates a firm legal obligation to restore money or other property received under a contract that is void or which has become void, or under an agreement which is void, to the person from whom it was received. The triggering conditions are (i) existence of a contract that is void or becomes void, (ii) receipt by the Defendant of money or property under that contract, and (iii) the failure of the contract to yield the expected consideration or any other circumstance that makes the contract void, such that the recipient is no longer entitled to retain the money or property. In the present case, the Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, paid Rs. 2,000,000 as an advance for a business transaction with the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, on {{DATE_OF_PAYMENT}} in {{LOCATION}}, and the Defendant defaulted, causing the business transaction to fail and resulting in a total failure of consideration; the Defendant has retained the Rs. 2,000,000 without lawful justification, and therefore is bound to restore the amount to the Plaintiff under the said provision. The payment is evidenced by Annexure B (Payment proof) and the underlying contract is evidenced by Annexure A (Agreement/Contract document); the Plaintiff has issued a legal notice to the Defendant, as reflected in Annexure C, and the Defendant has not produced any counter-claim or set-off that would extinguish the obligation to restitute. The liability to restitute is not affected by any alleged partial performance or by any purported waiver, and no forfeiture clause or other defence negates the central fact of total failure of consideration; accordingly, the Defendant is obliged to refund the entire amount of Rs. 2,000,000 with interest and costs as permitted by law. Defensively, the Defendant may contend that no part performance was rendered or that the agreement remains valid or that there is a counter-claim; however, the documentary record including Annexures A, B and C establishes (i) the existence of an agreement and the payment thereunder, (ii) the subsequent failure of the transaction due to the Defendant’s default, and (iii) the Plaintiff’s entitlement to restitution under Section 65, negating any such anticipated defenses.

blocking_issues.issue: Facts are drafted in a generic/skeleton manner and do not plead a clear chronological narrative (what the transaction was, specific obligations, what exactly failed, how/when termination occurred, and why consideration totally failed).
blocking_issues.fix: Rewrite the Facts section as a chronological narrative with transaction-specific particulars (using {{PLACEHOLDER}} fields where details are unknown), including: nature of proposed business transaction, key contractual obligations, payment mode and purpose of advance, performance milestones, specific default, termination/rescission, and continued non-refund.

blocking_issues.issue: Draft contains drafting-note language that must not appear in a filed pleading (e.g., references to a 'placeholder' within the interest paragraph).
blocking_issues.fix: Remove drafting-note language and keep only proper pleading text; use {{PLACEHOLDER}} fields without calling them 'placeholders' in the body.

blocking_issues.issue: Pendente lite interest period is pleaded incorrectly/inconsistently (interest is stated from {{FILING_DATE}} in one place but prayed from {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}}; also the end date is incorrectly tied to a cause-of-action date).
blocking_issues.fix: Align pendente lite interest to run from {{FILING_DATE}} till the date of decree, and future interest from date of decree till realization; ensure INTEREST section and PRAYER clause match.

blocking_issues.issue: Limitation and accrual pleading is internally inconsistent (mentions Article 47 accrual on failure date but then uses both {{DEFAULT_DATE}} and {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} as starting points).
blocking_issues.fix: For Article 47, plead one clear accrual/start date as the 'date of failure of consideration' (define it consistently as {{DATE_OF_FAILURE}} and tie it to the pleaded event, e.g., non-performance by due date and/or termination date). Remove conflicting alternate start dates.
blocking_issues.location: LIMITATION section; references in JURISDICTION/CAUSE OF ACTION/LEGAL BASIS

blocking_issues.issue: Section 65 Indian Contract Act trigger conditions are not adequately pleaded on the facts: Section 65 requires an agreement discovered void or a contract that becomes void; mere 'default' does not, by itself, establish that the contract became void/was avoided such that restitution under Section 65 is attracted.
blocking_issues.fix: Plead the factual basis for Section 65 applicability (e.g., the agreement stood terminated/rescinded/treated as at an end due to Defendant’s breach; consideration totally failed; Defendant is no longer entitled to retain the advance). Add a concise pleading of termination/rescission and total failure of consideration in the Facts and Legal Basis.
blocking_issues.location: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS (Section 65 paragraphs); FACTS/Cause of Action

non_blocking_issues.issue: Legal analysis is mixed into the Jurisdiction section (Section 65 discussion appears in Jurisdiction).
non_blocking_issues.fix: Move Section 65/restitution assertions out of Jurisdiction and confine Jurisdiction to territorial/pecuniary facts; keep legal propositions in Legal Basis.

non_blocking_issues.issue: Verification clause is generic and does not specify which paragraphs are verified on personal knowledge vs information/legal advice (often required in practice for clarity).
non_blocking_issues.fix: Amend verification to state paragraph ranges verified on personal knowledge and those based on records/legal advice/information, and state sources where applicable.

non_blocking_issues.issue: Prayer formatting is compressed into a single paragraph, reducing readability and increasing risk of clerical error.
non_blocking_issues.fix: Format prayer as separate numbered/sub-paragraph items with consistent dates and rates.

non_blocking_issues.issue: Use of inconsistent terminology ('complaint' for a civil plaint) may be considered imprecise.
non_blocking_issues.fix: Replace 'complaint'/'money recovery action' phrasing with 'plaint'/'suit' terminology consistently.

unsupported_statements.statement: Plaintiff and Defendant entered into a written Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} marked Annexure A.
unsupported_statements.reason: USER_REQUEST does not state that there is a written agreement; this must be confirmed from client records before filing.

unsupported_statements.statement: Plaintiff issued a legal notice on {{NOTICE_DATE}} (Annexure C) and Defendant replied on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} (Annexure D).
unsupported_statements.reason: USER_REQUEST does not mention any legal notice or reply; pleading these events without confirmation may be inaccurate.

unsupported_statements.statement: Annexure E and Annexure F contain acknowledgments/supporting documents.
unsupported_statements.reason: No context is provided about Annexure E/F; this is an ungrounded assertion unless documents exist and are identified.

unsupported_statements.statement: The Agreement contained an obligation 'to remedy the breach by returning the advance if performance could not be fulfilled.'
unsupported_statements.reason: USER_REQUEST does not provide contract terms; such a clause cannot be assumed.

unsupported_statements.statement: Defendant 'has not produced any counter-claim or set-off' and 'no forfeiture clause or other defence negates' the claim.
unsupported_statements.reason: These are defensive assertions not supported by any provided factual context/documents; they should be pleaded only if verified from pleadings/contract terms.
